title: How I Built This Portfolio (Part 1): Brief, Scope, and Architecture
excerpt: Part 1 of 7: the brief, constraints, and architecture decisions that defined this portfolio build from day one.

blocks.value: I wanted a portfolio that felt personal, fast, and maintainable. The core goals were simple: clear storytelling, great performance, and an architecture that could scale into projects, blog posts, and admin workflows without a rewrite.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1522542550221-31fd19575a2d?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1200
blocks.caption: Early architecture board: scope, routes, and feature boundaries

blocks.value: A portfolio is a product, not a brochure.
blocks.caption: Project principle

blocks.value: The initial information architecture had four main surfaces: Home, Projects, Blog, and Contact. I also planned an admin area from the beginning so content editing could move away from manual file updates later.

src/
  app/
  pages/
  components/
  content/
  hooks/
  services/
  utils/
My rule for architecture was: each folder should communicate intent. Components should stay mostly presentation-focused, while data access and auth concerns live in hooks and services.

export interface BlogPost {
  slug: string
  title: string
  excerpt: string
  publishedOn: string
  tags: string[]
  readMinutes: number
  blocks?: ContentBlock[]
}
Part 2: Vite + React setup and baseline structure

title: How I Built This Portfolio (Part 2): Vite + React Foundation
excerpt: Part 2 of 7: bootstrapping with Vite, TypeScript guardrails, and a structure that stayed clean as features grew.

blocks.value: I chose Vite for fast feedback loops. Early velocity matters because portfolio projects involve frequent visual iteration. TypeScript gave me a contract-first flow while designing content models and route payloads.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1555949963-aa79dcee981c?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1200
blocks.caption: Bootstrapping the frontend foundation with a fast dev loop

blocks.value:
npm create vite@latest
npm install
npm run dev
Before writing pages, I set strict defaults: clean import boundaries, reusable layout primitives, and project-wide linting so style drift would not creep in.

// src/main.tsx
import React from 'react'
import ReactDOM from 'react-dom/client'
import { BrowserRouter } from 'react-router-dom'
import { App } from './app/App'

ReactDOM.createRoot(document.getElementById('root')!).render(
  <React.StrictMode>
    <BrowserRouter>
      <App />
    </BrowserRouter>
  </React.StrictMode>,
)
Speed in development is a feature.
Part 3: Content model and multipage routing

title: How I Built This Portfolio (Part 3): Content Model and Multipage Routing
excerpt: Part 3 of 7: designing a content schema and route flow for projects, articles, and rich reusable blocks.

blocks.value: The turning point in this build was moving from plain strings to block-based content. That let me render text, code, media, and quotes consistently in both project pages and blog posts.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1504639725590-34d0984388bd?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1200
blocks.caption: Mapping dynamic routes and reusable content structures

blocks.value: Routes were intentionally explicit for clarity. I wanted predictable URLs and easy SEO indexing, so each main page got a stable route plus dynamic detail routes for project and blog slugs.

<Routes>
  <Route path="/" element={<HomePage />} />
  <Route path="/projects" element={<ProjectsPage />} />
  <Route path="/projects/:slug" element={<ProjectDetailPage />} />
  <Route path="/blog" element={<BlogPage />} />
  <Route path="/blog/:slug" element={<BlogPostPage />} />
  <Route path="*" element={<NotFoundPage />} />
</Routes>
To avoid fragile rendering bugs, every dynamic block passes through sanitization. That decision made rich content safer while keeping authoring flexibility.
A good content model removes page-specific hacks before they appear.
blocks.caption: Routing and content principle

blocks.value: Part 4: Design language, UI system, and motion

title: How I Built This Portfolio (Part 4): Design Language, Components, and Motion
excerpt: Part 4 of 7: shaping the visual language with spacing rhythm, reusable components, and restrained motion.

blocks.value: I treated the UI as a system, not a sequence of isolated pages. Spacing scales, typography, and surface styles were defined once and reused everywhere to keep the site cohesive.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1545239351-1141bd82e8a6?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1200
blocks.caption: Component-first design exploration with spacing and visual rhythm

blocks.value: The main reusable pieces were a page shell, header, footer, and card-like item blocks for projects and blog entries. That gave me layout consistency while letting each section have a distinct personality.
Consistency creates trust.
Part 5: Building the journal and content renderer

title: How I Built This Portfolio (Part 5): Journal Engine and Rich Content Renderer
excerpt: Part 5 of 7: building a flexible journal engine with rich blocks, code snippets, and safe rendering.

blocks.value: I wanted writing to feel expressive without introducing a full markdown parser pipeline. The compromise was a typed block renderer with safe HTML support for richer text formatting.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1515879218367-8466d910aaa4?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1200
blocks.caption: Testing mixed content blocks for a readable journal experience

blocks.value:
switch (block.type) { case 'text': return

case 'code': return 

{block.value}
case 'quote': return

{block.value}

default: return null }
The post model supports optional cover images, templates, and tags. That gives each article visual variation while preserving a shared reading structure for consistency.

{
  "slug": "my-post",
  "title": "Example",
  "tags": ["React", "Portfolio"],
  "template": "tech",
  "blocks": [{ "type": "text", "value": "Hello" }]
}
This structure also made content migration easier. Old long-form body arrays can still render, while newer posts use richer blocks.
The best blog engine is one that scales format without rewriting templates.
blocks.caption: Content system note

blocks.value: Part 6: Admin workflow, auth, and Supabase integration

title: How I Built This Portfolio (Part 6): Admin Workflow, Auth, and Supabase
excerpt: Part 6 of 7: adding admin auth and Supabase integration while preserving a resilient static fallback.

blocks.value: I designed the data layer with a practical hybrid strategy: static JSON as a safe default, Supabase when environment variables are present. This keeps local development friction low and production editing flexible.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1558494949-ef010cbdcc31?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1200
blocks.caption: Hybrid content flow: static fallback with cloud-backed admin updates

blocks.value: Admin routes are wrapped in a protected layer so only authenticated sessions can access create/update/delete flows. The public pages remain fully accessible and cache-friendly.

<Route
  path="/admin"
  element={
    <ProtectedRoute>
      <AdminLayout />
    </ProtectedRoute>
  }
/>
Graceful fallback is a feature, not a backup plan.
Part 7: Deployment, SEO, and the release checklist

title: How I Built This Portfolio (Part 7): Deployment, SEO, and Continuous Iteration
excerpt: Part 7 of 7: deployment, SEO, and the continuous iteration loop that keeps the portfolio sharp.

blocks.value: The last mile of this build was all about reliability: deploy confidence, crawlability, and maintainable release habits. I added redirects, sitemap generation, robots configuration, and structured data for article pages.
https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1454165804606-c3d57bc86b40?auto=format&fit=crop&q=80&w=1200
blocks.caption: Release checklist and analytics loop for continuous iteration

blocks.value:
npm run build
npm run preview
My post-release loop is simple: ship, observe, adjust. I monitor page weight, interaction flow, and content clarity, then refine sections that underperform.
A portfolio is never finished. It is versioned.
Read all posts in this series from the Journal page

title: Motorcycles and Code: Why I Ride
excerpt: Reflections on the parallels between riding a motorcycle and building resilient systems. Both require focus, precision, and respect for the unexpected.

blocks.value: I bought my first motorcycle three years ago as an antidote to spending too much time in front of screens. What started as weekend rides has become a meditation practice.
On a bike, you can't think about yesterday or tomorrow. You are only in this moment.
blocks.caption: Riding principle

blocks.value: The parallels to backend systems are obvious: you need situational awareness, you can't ignore warning signs, and planning ahead keeps you alive. Same lessons, different context.
I've learned as much about system reliability from riding in traffic as from any architecture textbook. Both demand humility.

title: A Rider's Guide to Bangalore Traffic
excerpt: Notes from my commute: how to stay safe, sane, and on schedule in one of India's busiest cities.

blocks.value: Bangalore traffic is often described as chaotic. It's not—it's simply different from Western traffic models. Once you understand the patterns, it's navigable.
The key is maintaining awareness, not speed.
Over the last two years, I've distilled a few rules: Assume invisible. Use mirror discipline. Find your steady pace. The commute should not define your day—it should clear your head.
Best riding happens early morning or late evening when the city breathes slower. Take a detour. The destination matters less than the ride.

title: On Coffee, Focus, and Sustainable Pace
excerpt: A short essay on the engineer's eternal struggle: how to maintain energy and clarity without burning out.

blocks.value: I've tried timers, sprints, and scheduled breaks. Nothing beats knowing your energy curve and protecting the hours when your mind is clearest.
Consistency beats intensity.
A sustainable pace looks ordinary. No all-nighters. No heroic sprints. Just steady, intentional work every day. The best engineers I know optimize for longevity, not for impressiveness.
